content.assessment: This proposal operates on the centralized admissions process itself—grades and entrance exams—which is squarely the governance mechanism the lens identifies as currently unchanged and consequential. However, it leaves the track structure and the 85%-selective-school landscape intact, and it does not address the separate admissions regime for church/private maintainers, a known site of institutional exemption from standard rules.
opportunities: Could be paired with monitoring of the centralized process to identify where grade/exam weighting most affects sorting outcomes
strengths: Targets the specific centralized mechanism (grades/entrance exams) documented as the current admissions basis, offering direct institutional leverage
Administrative compatibility is higher than structural reform since it works within, rather than against, the existing centralized application process
threats: Partial reach into only state-governed admissions leaves the institutional pathway used in the Nyíregyháza-type exemption cases unaddressed, risking displacement of selection pressure toward less-regulated maintainers
weaknesses: Does not alter the underlying track structure or the proportion of schools empowered to select, so system-wide sorting capacity remains at the 85% level

Church-maintained and private schools operate under separate rules exempt from standard admissions procedures, meaning a centrally reformed process would have limited reach into a documented segregation-relevant segment
